query: NgRx data flow end to end in Angular interview question

competitors.label: NgRx Guide
competitors.selectionReason: NgRx is the canonical source for Store, Effects, and selector concepts, so it is the official baseline for the data-flow topic.
competitors.winReason: NgRx docs are canonical across the whole platform, but our page is more immediately useful for an interview answer because it compresses the loop into one debug-friendly operational mental model.
theirStrengths: It is the authoritative reference for Store, Effects, Selectors, and the broader NgRx ecosystem.
theirGaps: It is broad and navigation-heavy rather than a single compact answer to the data-flow question.

realWorldUseCases: the UI dispatches intent, reducers compute immutable state, effects run async side effects beside that loop, and selectors expose a clean read model back to the template.
followUpConfusion: components dispatch actions for user intent, reducers compute next immutable state, selectors expose memoized read models, and templates render selector output.

realWorldUseCases: The official docs cover the whole NgRx platform, but our page is more immediately operational for explaining the end-to-end loop in one answer.
actionableExamples: NgRx docs still provide a large body of official examples and patterns, so the example comparison stays close.
followUpConfusion: Our page is more direct about why reducers stay pure and why effects live beside the loop rather than inside reducers.

actionableExamples: Add one compact action-to-effect-to-success-action trace so the loop is visible as data, not just prose.

competitors.label: GreatFrontEnd Angular Experienced Interview Questions
competitors.selectionReason: GreatFrontEnd has a directly relevant state-management section contrasting services and NgRx, including actions, reducers, and selectors.
competitors.winReason: GreatFrontEnd covers the choice between services and NgRx, but our page is more explicit about the full one-way loop and why effects and selectors exist as separate operational pieces.
theirStrengths: It is interview-oriented and already contrasts shared services with NgRx store architecture.
theirGaps: It is broader on state-management choice than on the full dispatch → reducer → effect → selector loop.

realWorldUseCases: the UI dispatches intent, reducers compute immutable state, effects run async side effects beside that loop, and selectors expose a clean read model back to the template.
followUpConfusion: reducers compute next immutable state, selectors expose memoized read models

realWorldUseCases: The experienced guide frames when to use NgRx versus a service, but our page is stronger on the mechanics of the full data loop.
actionableExamples: Its service-versus-store examples keep the example comparison close.
followUpConfusion: It is lighter on why effects sit outside reducers and why selectors are a read-model boundary instead of just getters.

actionableExamples: Add one failure-path example where an effect dispatches a loadFailed action and the selector drives a retry UI.

competitors.label: Angular University NgRx Crash Course
competitors.selectionReason: Angular University has a dedicated NgRx crash course, making it the strongest tutorial/reference competitor for reducers, effects, and selectors together.
theirStrengths: It is a focused long-form NgRx guide with more room for actions, reducers, selectors, and effects examples.
theirGaps: It is longer and less compact than a trivia answer.

realWorldUseCases: Pure reducer update vs effect-driven async update
actionableExamples: Compact trace you should be able to say out loud

realWorldUseCases: Angular University also ties NgRx to real application state flow, so the practical-use comparison stays close.
actionableExamples: As a crash course, it has more step-by-step NgRx examples than our compact page.
followUpConfusion: It already covers the main NgRx building blocks in depth, so the deeper follow-up comparison stays close.

realWorldUseCases: Add one example that contrasts a pure reducer update with an effect-driven async update in the same feature.
actionableExamples: Add one compact code trace showing action, reducer, effect, selector, and template usage together.
followUpConfusion: Keep one extra short note on failure actions and retry UI if the page later expands.

competitors.winReason: Our page now includes a reducer-versus-effect split, a compact end-to-end trace, and an explicit selector-as-read-model note, which makes it stronger on use-cases and worked examples while broader follow-up depth stays close.
